blocks.text: Signals, Signs & Pavement Markings

Traffic signs are grouped by shape and color so you can recognize them quickly. A red octagon always means STOP, a downward-pointing triangle means YIELD, and a yellow diamond warns of hazards ahead such as curves, merges, or pedestrian crossings. Orange signs mark construction or maintenance zones, and white regulatory signs tell you the law, such as speed limits and turn restrictions.

A steady red light means stop and remain stopped until the light turns green. A steady yellow light warns that the light is about to turn red, so prepare to stop if you can do so safely. A flashing red light is treated like a stop sign, while a flashing yellow light means slow down and proceed with caution. Green arrows give you a protected turn in the direction the arrow points.

Pavement markings guide and separate traffic. A solid yellow line marks the center of a two-way road and may not be crossed to pass, while a broken yellow line means passing is allowed when the way is clear. White lines separate lanes of traffic moving in the same direction, and a solid white line discourages lane changes. Always obey signals and markings together with posted signs.
Right-of-Way & Turning

Right-of-way rules tell you who should go first, but the law never gives anyone the right-of-way; it only states who must yield. At an intersection without signs or signals, yield to the vehicle on your right when you arrive at about the same time. When turning left, you must yield to oncoming traffic and to pedestrians in the crosswalk.

In Hawaii you may turn right on a red light after coming to a complete stop, unless a sign prohibits it, and only after yielding to pedestrians and cross traffic. Always stop behind the stop line or crosswalk before turning. Drivers must yield to pedestrians in marked and unmarked crosswalks and stop for any pedestrian who is in the driver's half of the roadway or approaching closely from the other half.

Emergency vehicles displaying flashing lights or sounding a siren always have the right-of-way; pull to the right edge of the road and stop until they pass. Hawaii's Move Over law also requires you to change lanes away from a stopped emergency or service vehicle with flashing lights, or, if you cannot move over safely, to slow to a reasonable and prudent speed as you pass.
Speed & Space Management

You must always drive at a speed that is reasonable and prudent for current conditions and never faster than the posted limit. Speed limits in business and residential districts are commonly 25 mph, while open highways and freeways carry higher posted limits. Slow down for rain, fog, heavy traffic, curves, and areas with pedestrians, since the posted limit is a maximum for ideal conditions only.

Following too closely is a leading cause of rear-end crashes. Use the three-second rule: pick a fixed object the vehicle ahead passes, then count, and if you reach it before counting to three you are too close. Increase your following distance to four seconds or more in rain, fog, or on slippery roads, and when being tailgated.

Manage the space all around your vehicle, not just ahead. Keep a cushion to the sides, avoid driving in another vehicle's blind spot, and leave yourself an escape path in traffic. Scan well ahead so you can adjust your speed and spacing smoothly rather than braking suddenly.
Passing & Lane Use

Pass only when it is legal and safe. Never cross a solid yellow line on your side of the road to pass, and never pass where signs prohibit it, near intersections, on curves, or when approaching a hill where you cannot see far enough ahead. Signal, check your mirrors and blind spot, and return to your lane only after you can see the passed vehicle in your rearview mirror.

On multi-lane roads, keep right except to pass, and use the left lane for passing slower traffic. Signal every lane change and every turn, and complete the move before you begin braking for a turn. Do not weave between lanes or straddle lane lines.

You may never pass a stopped school bus that is displaying flashing red lights and an extended stop arm; you must stop whether you are behind it or meeting it, until the lights stop flashing and the arm is withdrawn. Be especially alert for children near the bus.
Sharing the Road

Roads are shared with pedestrians, bicyclists, motorcyclists, mopeds, and large trucks, and many of them are harder to see and more easily injured than people in cars. Treat bicyclists as legal road users with the right to a full lane, give them room, and check carefully for them before turning or opening a door.

Motorcycles are small and can be hidden by blind spots or other vehicles, so look twice before changing lanes or turning. Allow motorcyclists a full lane width and increase your following distance behind them, since they can stop very quickly. Large trucks need much more room to stop and have large blind spots, so avoid lingering beside them and never cut in closely in front of one.

Always yield to pedestrians in crosswalks and watch for people who are blind or have low vision; a pedestrian using a white cane or guide dog has the right-of-way at all times. Slow down and stay alert in school zones, near bus stops, and in areas with heavy foot traffic.
Impaired & Distracted Driving

It is illegal to drive in Hawaii with a blood alcohol concentration of 0.08 percent or higher if you are 21 or older. Drivers under 21 are held to a zero-tolerance standard and may not drive with a BAC of 0.02 percent or more. Alcohol and many drugs, including prescription and over-the-counter medicines, slow reaction time and impair judgment, so the only safe choice is to drive sober.

By driving on Hawaii's roads you give implied consent to chemical testing for alcohol or drugs. Refusing a lawful breath, blood, or urine test results in an automatic license revocation, and impaired driving carries fines, license loss, and possible jail.

Distracted driving is also against the law. Hawaii bans holding a mobile electronic device while driving, and drivers under 18 may not use any mobile device, even hands-free. Texting and other distractions take your eyes, hands, and mind off the task, so put the phone away before you start driving.
Driving in Adverse Conditions

Rain, fog, and glare reduce both traction and visibility. Hawaii law requires you to turn on your headlights whenever conditions limit visibility and whenever your windshield wipers are in continuous use, in addition to from sunset to sunrise. Headlights help you see and, just as importantly, help others see you.

Slow down on wet roads and increase your following distance, because tires can lose grip and hydroplane on standing water. If your vehicle begins to hydroplane, ease off the gas, keep the steering steady, and avoid braking hard. Roads are most slippery during the first few minutes of rain when water mixes with oil on the surface.

In fog, use low-beam headlights rather than high beams, since high beams reflect back and reduce visibility. Reduce speed, use the right edge line or pavement markings as a guide, and avoid stopping on the roadway. If visibility becomes too poor to continue, pull completely off the road and turn on your hazard lights.
Parking & Rules of the Road

Park only where it is legal and where your vehicle will not block traffic, crosswalks, fire hydrants, or driveways. Never park in a space reserved for persons with disabilities unless you have the proper permit or plates. When you leave your vehicle, set the parking brake, and on a hill turn your wheels so the vehicle will not roll into traffic.

When parking facing downhill, turn your front wheels toward the curb so the vehicle rolls into it if it moves. When facing uphill with a curb, turn the wheels away from the curb and let the vehicle roll back gently against it; with no curb, always turn the wheels toward the edge of the road.

Obey all curb-color and signed parking restrictions, and do not stop or stand in places that block intersections or the flow of traffic. Before pulling out of a parking space, signal and check mirrors and blind spots for approaching vehicles, bicyclists, and pedestrians.
Licensing & Permits

Hawaii uses a graduated driver licensing system for new drivers. You may apply for an instruction permit at age 15 and a half after passing the written knowledge test and a vision check. While holding the instruction permit you must always be accompanied by a licensed driver at least 21 years old seated in the front passenger seat, and between 11:00 p.m. and 5:00 a.m. that supervising driver must be next to you.

Before moving to the next stage you must hold the instruction permit for at least 180 days and complete the required supervised practice driving, including practice at night, then pass a road test. A provisional license then lets you drive on your own with restrictions until you meet the requirements for a full license.

Provisional license holders may not drive between 11:00 p.m. and 5:00 a.m. unless accompanied by a licensed parent or guardian, and may not carry more than one passenger under 18 who is not a household member unless a licensed adult is supervising. These restrictions exist because new drivers face the highest crash risk at night and with teen passengers.
Penalties & Enforcement

Hawaii enforces its seat belt law as a primary offense, meaning an officer can stop and cite you for a belt violation alone. Everyone in the vehicle, in both the front and back seats, must be properly restrained. Drivers are responsible for making sure passengers are buckled up.

Children under 4 must ride in an approved child safety seat, and children ages 4 up to 10 must use a child seat or booster seat unless they are taller than 4 feet 9 inches. Violations of the child restraint law carry substantial fines and may require attending a safety class.

Traffic violations add up and can lead to fines, points, license suspension or revocation, and higher insurance costs. Driving impaired, refusing a chemical test, fleeing a crash, or repeatedly violating the law brings the most serious penalties, including possible jail time and long-term loss of driving privileges.
